actions.thought: KJo is a strong starting hand 3-handed. As the first to act on the Button, opening with a raise is standard to put pressure on the blinds and take the initiative. A raise to 2.5x the big blind (250 chips) is a solid sizing to accomplish this.

Kh4h is a weak hand to defend from the Small Blind against a button open. Playing out of position with this hand is disadvantageous, and given it is the first hand of the tournament, I prefer to fold and preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I have Ah 7c in the Big Blind. The Button (Pro) raised to 250, and the Small Blind folded. I need to call 150 more to see the flop in a pot that will be 550. This gives me excellent pot odds of nearly 3.7:1. Ah 7c is a strong enough hand to defend against a standard Button opening range, as it has good high-card value and some playability. I'll call to see the flop rather than 3-betting, which keeps the pot size manageable and allows me to see how the board develops.

On a King-high board like Kd-4c-3h, the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) has a significant range advantage as they have all the strong Kings and overpairs in their range. My Ace-high (Ah 7c) has limited value here and did not connect with the flop. Being out of position, the standard play is to check to the initial raiser and see how they react. I will evaluate my next move based on their bet sizing, but for now, checking is the most sound strategic option.

I hit top pair on a very dry flop (3h Kd 4c). Since my opponent checked to me, this is a clear spot for a value bet. I want to extract chips from weaker Kings, underpairs, or gutshot straight draws that might call. A smaller bet sizing of around 35-45% of the pot works well on dry, disconnected boards like this to keep worse hands in the pot.

The board is King-high (Kd 3h 4c), which significantly favors the Button's opening range. My hand, Ah 7c, has not improved and lacks any significant draws or backdoor equity. While Ace-high has some showdown value, calling out of position against a continuation bet on this texture is generally losing, as I will likely face further aggression on later streets and have no clear way to improve to the best hand. Folding is the most disciplined play here to preserve my stack.
With 5-9 offsuit in a 3-handed game, this hand lacks the strength to profitably open from the button. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve stack size early in the tournament.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Four offsuit (Ad 4s). Flashlight on the Button has folded, leaving only the Big Blind (Pro) to act. In a blind vs. blind situation, an Ace is a relatively strong starting hand. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially win it immediately, or play with initiative post-flop if called.

Flash raised 3x from the Small Blind, which typically represents a wide stealing range, though it could include strong hands. King-Queen offsuit is a strong hand that dominates many of the hands in a Small Blind opening range. While a 3-bet is a viable option to apply pressure, calling allows us to keep his weaker bluffs and dominated hands in the pot while leveraging our positional advantage post-flop.

I have flopped top pair with a weak kicker on a very dry board (Ac-Td-2s). As the pre-flop aggressor, a continuation bet is standard here to extract value from weaker hands like a Ten, middle pocket pairs, or gutshot straight draws (like KJ or QJ). Since the board is uncoordinated, a smaller sizing of 200 (1/3 pot) is sufficient to maintain initiative and get information without over-committing against a potentially better Ace.

The opponent makes a small continuation bet of 1/3 pot on an A-T-2 rainbow board. We hold Ks Qh, giving us only a gutshot straight draw (4 outs to a Jack) and two overcards that might be reverse-implied if the opponent holds an Ace. Without any backdoor flush draw and only a weak gutshot, we don't have enough direct equity to call profitably. Given the opponent's pre-flop aggression, their range connects well with an Ace-high board, making floating or bluff-raising too risky. Folding is the disciplined and optimal play.

Q4o is a weak hand even in a three-handed game from the button. It lacks connectivity and high-card strength, making it difficult to play post-flop. Given the early stage of the tournament, I will fold and wait for a more advantageous starting hand.

With Q6 suited in the small blind after the button folds, we have a great opportunity to attack the big blind. Q6s is strong enough to play aggressively in a blind versus blind scenario. By raising, we can win the pot uncontested pre-flop or take the betting initiative into the flop with a hand capable of flopping strong pairs or flush draws.

I am in the Big Blind with 3h-9h, which is a weak hand with low playability. Even though I am getting 2:1 pot odds, calling a 3x raise out of position with this specific holding is generally negative expected value. I prefer to fold and conserve my stack for a more advantageous situation.

With 73 offsuit on the button, this is a clear fold. The hand has almost no playability, extremely low equity against any reasonable calling range, and no straight or flush potential that warrants entering the pot. Preserving stack size early in the tournament is important.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Five offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. Even though I am in a heads-up situation against the Big Blind after the Button folded, there is no reason to invest further chips into the pot with such a poor hand. Folding is the most disciplined play to protect my stack.

Q2 offsuit is a very weak hand with poor playability, even in a short-handed game. Opening or calling with such a weak hand from the button is generally suboptimal. I should fold and wait for a more profitable spot.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Deuce offsuit (J2o). This is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. While it's only 50 more to call, J2o realization of equity is low against the Big Blind's range. Given the early stage of the tournament and the weak nature of the hand, folding is the most prudent play to preserve chips for better opportunities.

85o is a very weak hand, even from the button in a 3-handed game. It doesn't have much post-flop equity and is not a strong candidate for a steal against competent opponents. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Action is folded to me in the Small Blind. I hold 76 offsuit, which is a weak and marginal hand. Playing this out of position against the Big Blind is generally unprofitable over the long run. I will fold and conserve my stack for a more advantageous spot.

We are first to act on the Button with A3o. This is a reasonable hand to open for a steal, especially 3-handed. An open to 2.5x (250) applies pressure to the blinds while keeping our risk relatively low.

I am in the Small Blind with suited connectors (9c-Jc), which have decent playability and equity. While I am out of position against the Button's raise, the pot odds are acceptable to call and see a flop. I prefer calling over 3-betting here to keep the pot size manageable while playing out of position, and folding seems too tight given the hand strength and stack depth.

I am in the Big Blind holding King-Deuce of hearts. The Button raised to 250 and the Small Blind called. I need to call 150 more into a pot that will be 750, giving me 5:1 pot odds. K2s has reasonable playability in a multi-way pot, especially with the flush potential, and the price is too good to fold. I will call to see a flop.

I have middle pair on a board that contains a queen and potential flush draws. Leading out in a three-way pot from out of position against the pre-flop aggressor is not advisable. Checking allows me to see how the betting develops while keeping the pot manageable, and I have enough equity to potentially call a reasonable bet depending on sizing.

I have King-high with no current draws on a board of 6d-9d-Qh. The pre-flop raiser (Pro) is still to act behind me. Flashlight checked from the Small Blind. Given I have no hand strength or equity, I will check and likely fold to any significant aggression from the Button.

I have Ace-high with a backdoor diamond draw on a somewhat coordinated board (6d 9d Qh) in a multiway pot. Both opponents checked to me, but c-betting into two players with no made hand and weak draws is risky. Checking behind allows me to see a free turn card and re-evaluate, keeping the pot small when I have low equity.

The flop checked around, indicating relative weakness from everyone. My pair of nines is a marginal hand that is likely ahead of random bluffs or weaker pairs but vulnerable to any Queen or overpair. Betting into two opponents here would likely force out hands I beat while getting called only by better hands. I will check again to control the pot size and keep the hand as cheap as possible to reach a showdown.

I have King-high with no draws on a board of 6d-9d-Qh-5s. The flop went check-check-check, and the Small Blind (Flashlight) has checked the turn as well. While Pro (the pre-flop raiser) also checked the flop, I have no real showdown value and very little equity if I am called on a bluff. Checking is the most prudent move here to see if I can get to a cheap showdown or a free river, as I have no reason to build a pot with this hand.

The turn card is a 5s, changing little. Both opponents checked again, indicating weakness. However, I still only have Ace-high with a weak kicker and no draw. Betting as a delayed c-bet bluff is an option, but into two opponents, someone might have a pair or a draw they are sticky with. Checking behind gives a free river card where I can either hit an Ace or evaluate if a bluff makes sense on the end, while risking no extra chips.

The board has checked through on both the flop and the turn, which typically indicates marginal hand strength from the opponents. My pair of nines has some showdown value, but it is vulnerable to better pairs and random straights or higher cards. Given that nobody has shown strength throughout the hand, betting now would likely only be called by hands that beat me (like a Queen or a pair of Tens), while checking allows me to realize my equity and reach the showdown for free. Checking is the most prudent play.

The board has been checked through the flop and the turn by all three players. The river is a Ten of hearts, completing a very marginal board (6-9-Q-5-T). My King-high has almost no showdown value, but in a pot that has been checked down this far, it's possible that someone has a weak pair or even Ace-high. Since there are still two players to act (myself and Pro behind me), and Flashlight has checked for the third time, I will check as well. Bluffing here into two opponents is risky, and I'm hoping to either win a very cheap showdown or simply fold if Pro decides to bet.

With Ace-high on a board of 6d 9d Qh 5s Th, and both opponents checking all three post-flop streets, it's highly likely they are either holding weak pairs or busted draws (like missed diamonds or straight draws). While a bluff could potentially fold out a better Ace-high or a marginal pair, it carries unnecessary risk. My Ace-high actually has decent showdown value against missed draws. Checking behind ensures I don't commit any more chips while taking a free showdown in a pot where I might already be ahead.

6-2 offsuit is extremely weak, disconnected, and low-ranked, offering minimal equity in any scenario. As the first to act on the button, playing this hand is not profitable. Folding is the standard disciplined play.

I am in the Small Blind with J8o. The Button has folded. While J8o is a playable hand in many heads-up situations, 'Pro' in the Big Blind has shown a tendency to be aggressive and active in previous hands. Playing Jack-high out of position against a capable opponent is generally unfavorable. Given the stack sizes are nearly equal and it is early in the tournament, I will opt for a conservative fold rather than risking chips in a marginal spot where I will be at a positional disadvantage for the rest of the hand.

I am on the button with 93o. While the button is a strong position to steal from, 93o is significantly below the threshold for a standard opening range in a 3-handed game. With stacks being nearly 100BB deep, it is better to wait for a more playable hand than to commit chips with a bottom-tier starting hand. I will fold.

K5 offsuit is a relatively weak hand, even in a Blind vs. Blind scenario. Playing out of position against the Big Blind with a disconnected hand that has poor playability post-flop is not optimal. Folding is the safest and most standard decision to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

KTo is a very standard opening hand from the button in a 3-handed game. I have position on the blinds and want to either pick up the dead money or play a pot in position with a decent broadway hand. I will raise to 2.5x (250).

My hand, Ten-Three offsuit, is extremely weak and has poor playability. Furthermore, being in the Small Blind places me out of position for the rest of the hand against the Button's opening range. Defending against a raise with such a marginal holding is mathematically unprofitable. I will fold to preserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Ten offsuit. The Button (Pro) raised to 250, and the Small Blind folded. QTo is a strong hand to defend the Big Blind with in a 3-handed game, as it has significant equity against a standard Button opening range. The pot odds are favorable, requiring me to call 150 more into a 400 pot (approx 27% equity needed). Calling allows me to see a flop and realize my equity without bloating the pot out of position.

I have top pair (Queens) on a dry, paired board (5-5-Q). This is a very strong hand in a heads-up pot against the Button's opening range. Checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard and most effective play here, as it allows Pro to make a continuation bet with a wider range of hands, including bluffs and weaker pairs, which I can then comfortably call or raise.

The flop is extremely dry and paired, which strongly favors my pre-flop raising range. Although I only have King-high, a small continuation bet of around 1/3 to 1/2 pot will fold out a large portion of the Big Blind's defending range that missed this board entirely, allowing me to pick up the pot without resistance.

I have top pair on a very dry, paired board (5-5-Q). Pro's small continuation bet of 200 into 550 is a standard play on this type of texture. Given that the board is quite static with few draws, raising here would likely only isolate me against better hands like AQ, KQ, or trips (5x), while folding out his air and bluffs. Calling is the most profitable line as it keeps Pro's entire range in, allows me to extract value from his bluffs on future streets, and keeps the pot manageable since I have a mediocre kicker (Ten).

I have top pair on a very static board. The 6c on the turn is a blank that doesn't significantly change the hand's texture. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, checking is the most effective play. It allows Pro to continue with a wide range of bluffs or to check back with hands that have some showdown value. I have a strong enough hand to call most standard bets, but leading here would likely fold out his bluffs and isolate myself against stronger hands like better Queens or trip fives.

The turn brings a 6c, which is a brick. Flash called my flop continuation bet on a dry, paired board, meaning their range is heavily weighted toward a Queen, a medium pocket pair, or a stubborn Ace-high. Since I only have King-high with no draw, firing a second barrel here is unlikely to force a fold from a pair, and I have very little equity if called. Checking back allows me to take a free river card, realize any equity I might have by hitting a King, and keep the pot controlled.

The river Ace of Spades is a significant scare card. Although I held top pair on the flop and turn with Queen-Ten, Pro's range as the pre-flop aggressor contains many Ace-X hands that have now improved to the best hand. Pro checked back on the turn, which often indicates a hand with showdown value that didn't want to build a pot, such as a weak Queen or an Ace that was waiting for the river. If I bet now, I am unlikely to be called by worse hands, and I would likely only be called or raised by hands that beat me (like an Ace or a five). By checking, I can reach showdown cheaply against hands I still beat (like JJ-77 or a weaker Queen) and potentially catch a bluff if Pro decides to represent the Ace.

The river brings an Ace, which hits a large portion of Flash's flop floating range (such as Ax hands). Since he called the flop continuation bet, his range consists of Qx, medium pocket pairs, and Ace-high floats. While a bluff might fold out some smaller pocket pairs, it gets snapped off by any Ax that just improved to top pair, and he might stubbornly call with Qx. King-high might occasionally win at showdown against busted floats like JTs or J9s. The safest option is to check back, realize any marginal showdown value, and avoid throwing chips away on a risky bluff.

With J-T offsuit in a 3-handed game, this is a playable hand, especially in position on the button. Since the pot is currently 150 (50 SB + 100 BB), calling the 100 chips is a standard way to see a flop and keep the pot control while evaluating the board texture.

I am in the Small Blind with A4o. The Button (Flashlight) has limped into the pot, which often signals a weak or wide range in a 3-handed game. While A4o is not a powerhouse, it is frequently the best hand in this scenario. I will raise to 400 to exert pressure on the limper and the Big Blind (Pro). This size allows me to take down the pot immediately or isolate the limper while remaining disciplined enough to fold if I face a significant 3-bet from Pro, who has shown aggressive tendencies in previous hands.

I have 9s 5s in the Big Blind. Flashlight limped on the Button and Flash raised to 400 in the Small Blind. This is a weak suited one-gapper facing a substantial raise and a potential trap or call from the limper. It's an easy fold pre-flop.

I am facing a 3-bet from the Small Blind after I limped/called the initial blind. J-10 offsuit is a marginal hand that performs poorly against a standard 3-bet range, which often includes higher Broadway cards or pocket pairs that dominate me. Calling 300 chips into this pot provides marginal pot odds, and being out of position against a 3-better is not ideal for this hand's post-flop playability. Folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack for better spots.

I am on the button with 7-2 suited, which is widely considered one of the weakest starting hands in poker despite being suited. While position is an advantage, the risk of getting into a difficult spot post-flop with such low equity is high. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

With K7 offsuit in the small blind after the button folds, we have a decent high-card hand that plays reasonably well heads-up. Raising puts immediate pressure on the big blind to defend, allowing us to frequently win the pot uncontested.

I hold pocket Jacks, which is a premium hand in a heads-up scenario. Against the Small Blind's raise, 3-betting is the optimal move to seize initiative, build value, and protect my hand. A raise to 900 is an appropriate sizing to apply pressure and define the range.

I opened K7 offsuit from the small blind as a standard steal attempt against the big blind. However, Flashlight 3-bet to 900. K7 offsuit is much too weak to defend against a 3-bet, especially out of position, so the optimal play here is to simply fold and surrender the pot.

Pocket Kings is the second-best starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Being first to act on the Button in a 3-handed game, I want to build the pot and get value from worse hands. I will open to my standard raise size of 2.5x the big blind (250 chips) to disguise my hand strength and encourage calls from the blinds.

I am in the small blind with Ace-Eight offsuit. While Ace-Eight is not a premium holding, it has significant equity against a standard Button opening range. Given that Pro is playing aggressively, I have enough pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot of 400) to justify a call. Folding is too tight, and a 3-bet could isolate me against a range that might include hands that dominate A8o, or trigger a 4-bet that forces me to fold. Playing in position after a call is not ideal, but the hand strength is sufficient to see a flop.

I am holding a small pocket pair (22) in the big blind. The button raised to 250 and the small blind called. I am getting excellent pot odds (4:1) to call and see a flop, looking to hit a set. With deep stacks, the implied odds make this a standard call to see if I can improve on the flop.

The board is paired with 9-9, and my Ace-high has very little showdown value against two opponents. With no pair and no significant draw, betting into two opponents would be ill-advised and likely result in a fold to any aggression. Checking is the standard play to maintain pot control and evaluate the turn for free if it checks around.

I am in the big blind with a small pocket pair (22) on a 9s-9c-Qd flop. This is a very dry board that doesn't favor my hand. The Small Blind has checked to me. I have no piece of this board, and there are two players behind me (the Small Blind who checked and the Button who was the pre-flop aggressor). Given that my pair is extremely vulnerable and the board texture is unfavorable, checking is the most prudent action. If there's a bet, I will likely fold, as my hand has very little equity to improve against a range that includes Qx, 9x, or higher pairs.

With pocket Kings on a 9-9-Q board, I have a very strong overpair. Both opponents have checked to me, making this a prime spot to bet for value and protection. I want to extract chips from top pair hands like AQ, KQ, QJ, or QT, as well as pocket pairs like JJ or TT, and charge straight draws like JT. A bet of about half the pot is standard here, as it gets value from worse hands while keeping the pot manageable in case someone happens to hold a 9.

The board is paired with 9s-9c-Qd, and the button (Pro) has shown aggression by betting 350 into two players. My Ace-high hand has no showdown value and virtually no equity against a range that likely includes a Queen, a Nine, or pocket pairs. Calling is mathematically and strategically unsound here, as I am out of position and have no draw. Folding is the optimal play to minimize losses and preserve my stack.

I am holding pocket deuces on a board of 9s-9c-Qd. I have no piece of this board, and my hand is extremely vulnerable to any overcards or any pair. The pre-flop aggressor (Pro) has placed a continuation bet of nearly half the pot. While I am getting roughly 4:1 odds to call, my hand has very little equity to improve (only two deuces left in the deck) and I am out of position. Calling here would likely result in me having to fold to further aggression on the turn or river anyway, as almost any card that comes is an overcard to my pair. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

7-8 offsuit is a marginal hand with limited potential. As the Button in a 3-max game, it is best to play disciplined poker and fold weak hands rather than inflating the pot without a strong starting hand. This conserves the stack for more advantageous situations.

I have a premium hand (Ace-King suited). Since the action folded to me in the Small Blind, I will raise to build the pot and take control of the hand against the Big Blind. A 3x raise is a standard and strong play in this position.
I am in the big blind facing a raise from the small blind with 9d 4s, which is an extremely weak offsuit hand. The best play is to simply fold.

I am on the button with 8s 3c, which is an extremely weak hand. Even in a 3-handed game, this hand is well below the threshold for a standard opening raise. I will fold to preserve my stack and wait for a better opportunity.

With pocket Kings in the small blind and the button having folded, I hold a premium starting hand. I want to build the pot and extract value from the big blind. Raising to 300 chips (3 big blinds) is a standard size that encourages action from worse hands while punishing any limping or defending ranges.

I am in the big blind with pocket nines, which is a strong hand in a 3-handed game, especially against an opponent's open from the small blind. Calling is the standard play here to keep the pot size manageable and see a flop. Raising could potentially force a fold from worse hands or isolate me against a better range, but calling maintains a high equity range and allows me to assess the flop texture.

With pocket Kings on a 4h-4d-Qd board, I have a very strong overpair. The board presents some diamond flush draws and a Queen, which the big blind could easily have in their defending range. Betting half the pot (300 chips) is optimal to extract value from Qx hands, worse pocket pairs, and flush draws, while keeping the pot manageable in case my opponent happens to hold a rare 4 or QQ.

I hold pocket nines on a relatively dry board of 4-4-Q. The opponent's bet is a standard continuation bet on a paired board. My hand currently functions as a bluff-catcher against air and as a strong hand against lower pairs or high-card hands. Raising here is likely to isolate myself against hands that have me beat (like a Queen or a higher pocket pair) or force a fold from hands I am currently dominating. Calling is the most profitable play as it keeps the pot manageable, maintains my equity, and allows me to see a turn card before committing more chips.

The Jack on the turn introduces a few hands that now beat me, such as QJ making two pair or a slow-played JJ hitting a set. However, my pocket Kings still comfortably beat the vast majority of the big blind's calling range, which consists heavily of top-pair hands (AQ, QT, Q9) and various diamond flush draws or straight draws (like KT or T9). Betting half the pot serves to extract value from those weaker made hands while simultaneously charging the draws and denying them a free river card.

The board texture is 4-4-Q-J. My pocket nines act as a bluff-catcher against the opponent's range. While I am behind any Queen, Jack, or four, the opponent could also be betting with overcards or smaller pairs like 88, 77, or 55. Given the pot odds of 3:1 (600 to win a 1800 pot), calling is a mathematically sound decision to reach showdown or re-evaluate on the river. Raising would force out hands I beat and isolate me against hands that have me crushed, so I will stick with a call.

The 7d on the river completes the obvious diamond flush draw that has been present since the flop. Many hands in my opponent's calling range, such as flush draws, QJ, or even an elusive 4x, now beat my pocket Kings. The hands I actually beat, like AQ or a weaker Qx without a diamond, will likely fold to a sizable bet but will happily check behind. Betting here risks getting raised or value-owning myself against improved hands. By checking, I practice pot control, allow my opponent the opportunity to bluff with missed straight draws like T9 or KT, and can safely evaluate their action.

The board is 4-4-Q-J-7. I hold pocket nines. My opponent (Pro) checked on the river. My pocket nines have decent showdown value against missed draws or air, but they lose to any Queen, Jack, or four. Betting would be a mistake because I will only be called by better hands that have me beat. Checking behind is the optimal play to take my hand to showdown and potentially win against the opponent's missed bluffs.
Queen-Ten suited is a solid starting hand, especially from the Button. Raising to 2.5x is a standard open to either steal the blinds or play a pot in position with a hand that plays well post-flop.

Ace-Ten suited is a strong hand that holds up well against the wide opening range of an aggressive opponent on the button. Since I am in the small blind and out of position, I prefer 3-betting to take control of the hand and build the pot with initiative, rather than just calling and playing out of position.

I am holding Jack-Four offsuit in the Big Blind. The Button (Pro) opened with a raise, and the Small Blind (Flashlight) 3-bet to 850. This is a very weak hand to be involved in a multi-way or 3-bet pot. I should fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Facing a substantial 3.4x 3-bet from the Small Blind, QTs is too weak and easily dominated by hands in their 3-betting range (like AQ, KQ, or high pocket pairs) to justify calling. Although we have position, folding is the disciplined choice to avoid reverse implied odds and preserve our stack.

I am on the button with King-Queen offsuit, which is a strong starting hand. Since no one has entered the pot before me, I should open the action. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) allows me to take control of the hand, potentially pick up the blinds, or play a slightly larger pot with the benefit of position.

I have been dealt Pocket Aces, the strongest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Flashlight raised from the button, which is often a wide range. I must 3-bet here to build value and narrow the field. Raising to 1000 represents a strong sizing out of the small blind, putting pressure on both the button and the big blind while building a substantial pot for my premium hand.

My hand is 6s 3d, which is an extremely weak, unsuited hand with no connectivity. Facing an open raise from the Button and a 3-bet from the Small Blind, I have zero incentive to get involved. Folding is the only correct and safe play.

Facing a 3-bet from the small blind when I am on the button with King-Queen offsuit is a standard scenario. While I am behind premium pocket pairs and Ace-King, my hand is strong enough to call given the positional advantage I hold. Folding would be too passive, and 4-betting is unnecessarily risky. I will call the 700 chip raise to see a flop and utilize my position to navigate the rest of the hand.

I hold Pocket Aces and have flopped an overpair along with the nut flush draw on a [4c 7c 5s] board. Although this board texture can be coordinated and favor a caller's range with potential sets or straights, my hand is extremely strong and needs to bet for value and protection. I will lead with a continuation bet of approximately half the pot to charge any draws and get value from hands like 88-JJ or various 7x/6x holdings.

Facing a bet of 1,000 into a 2,100 pot on a board of 4c-7c-5s, I have King-Queen offsuit with the Queen of clubs. This provides me with two overcards and a backdoor flush draw. Given the pot odds of roughly 3.1:1, I am getting the right price to call and potentially realize my equity on the turn or bluff if the situation arises. Folding would be too conservative, and raising as a semi-bluff against an active opponent out of position is risky. Calling is the most balanced and profitable approach.

The turn card is a Four of Hearts, which pairs the lowest card on the board. This is generally a safe card for my Pocket Aces, as it's unlikely Flashlight called a 3-bet pre-flop with many hands containing a four, except perhaps A4s or 54s. I still hold the overpair and the nut flush draw (Ac). I want to continue extracting value from pairs like 88-JJ and lower flush draws. A bet of 1500 (roughly 36% of the pot) is sized to keep Flashlight's range wide enough to call with marginal hands while still building the pot. If they raise, I have significant equity and will likely commit my stack.

The board texture is 4c-7c-5s-4h, which is low and coordinated, making it highly likely that my King-Queen high is currently behind. The opponent, Flash, has consistently shown strength by betting both the flop and the turn. My current equity is quite low, and the pot odds of approximately 3.7:1 do not justify a call with only overcards that may not even be clean outs. Given my healthy stack of 7450 chips, there is no need to make a marginal, high-variance call in this position. Folding is the most disciplined and professional decision, preserving my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the button with 9d 2h, which is a very weak hand. Given my position and the strength of my opponents, there is no reason to play this hand or attempt a bluff steal. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I am in the Small Blind with 38s after the Button folds. This is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Attempting a steal or completing the bet is not worth the risk here given the weakness of the cards. The standard and safest play is to fold.
I am on the Button with T6o, a very weak offsuit hand with low equity and poor playability. It is standard to fold this garbage pre-flop.

The hand 7-2 offsuit is the weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Despite the small cost to complete the big blind, playing such a weak hand out of position against the big blind is strategically disadvantageous and would likely lead to losing more chips in the long run. Folding is the disciplined play here.

Since I am out of position in the big blind, I want to start building the pot for value immediately while denying odds to any potential draws or weaker hands. Betting approximately half the pot (350 into 650) is a solid value bet that allows me to get value from weaker Aces or pairs, and helps clarify where I stand against the opponent's range.

I raised pre-flop with 10d 5d as a standard button steal attempt. The flop came Ah 3d 8c, which is quite favorable for the big blind's calling range, and I have missed completely, possessing only a weak backdoor diamond draw. Flashlight has led out (donk bet) for 350 into a 650 pot. Given that the board is Ace-high and I have no significant equity or a pair, there is little reason to continue. I will fold and preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

Pocket Nines (9d 9s) is a strong starting hand, especially 3-handed and on the Button. Raising pre-flop is standard to build the pot, take the betting initiative, and potentially steal the blinds or isolate a single caller.

Holding A7o in the small blind against a button open is a standard defensive play to see a flop. While playing out of position is challenging, the Ace provides some equity and useful card removal. Calling is the most appropriate action to maintain a reasonable pot size without overcommitting with a marginal hand.

I am in the Big Blind holding Jack-Six offsuit. The Button (Pro) raised to 250, and the Small Blind (Flashlight) called. I have a healthy stack and would rather avoid a high-variance, low-equity spot where I will likely be forced to fold on most flops or find myself dominated if I hit a pair. I will fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.

I hold top pair with a weak kicker on an Ace-high board. Being out of position as the small blind, checking is the prudent play to allow the pre-flop aggressor to act, maintain pot control, and keep my stack secure against a potentially stronger hand.

The flop brings two overs (Ace and King) and a flush draw, hitting the pre-flop caller's range very heavily. Betting with third pair and a backdoor flush draw would essentially be a bluff against a range that connects well with this board. Checking behind for pot control and giving up the betting initiative is the best and safest option here.

With top pair and a weak kicker on a board that has been checked down on the flop and turn, my hand has moderate showdown value but is vulnerable. Checking is the most prudent action to control the pot size and avoid value-betting myself into a stronger hand that might have checked behind. This play allows me to safely reach a showdown without committing unnecessary chips with a marginal hand.

Flashlight has checked again on the turn. The board still has an Ace and a King, which are dangerous overcards to our pocket Nines. Betting here is unlikely to get value from worse hands, as only stronger hands like A-x or K-x and strong draws will call. Furthermore, a bet won't easily fold out a slow-played top pair. Checking behind maintains pot control and allows us to see a free river, aiming for a cheap showdown.

I hold top pair with a 7 kicker, giving me two pair (Aces and Fours) with the board. Since the flop and turn were checked down, my hand has some showdown value, but it is vulnerable to any better Ace. Checking is the most prudent action to control the pot size and safely reach a showdown without risking unnecessary chips against a stronger holding.

The board contains two major overcards, an Ace and a King. Flashlight has checked all three streets, suggesting they likely have a weak holding such as a missed draw, a small pocket pair, or a weak top/second pair that they are playing cautiously. While our pocket Nines have decent showdown value against missed draws and smaller pairs, betting here is a classic 'way ahead / way behind' situation. If we bet, worse hands will fold and we will primarily be called by better hands like an Ace or a King. Checking behind ensures a safe showdown to realize our hand's equity without unnecessary risk.
With Ace-seven offsuit on the button, I have a strong enough hand to open, especially in a three-handed game. I will raise to 250 to attempt to steal the blinds or gain initiative in the hand.

Folding is the most prudent play to conserve chips for better spots.

K2o is a weak, disconnected hand that plays poorly out of position. Facing a 2.5x open from the Button, it is firmly in the folding range, as it lacks post-flop playability and faces serious reverse implied odds when hitting a King.

I am on the button with Q9o, which is a strong enough hand to open in a three-handed game. Standard play from the button is to raise to 2.5x or 3x the big blind to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot early. A raise to 300 has been my standard size.

With Ace-Jack suited in the Small Blind against a Button open, we have a premium hand that plays very well as a 3-bet. 3-betting allows us to potentially win the pot right here, isolates the Button if they call, and takes the betting initiative which is crucial when playing out of position. I am in the Big Blind with 7-4 offsuit, which is an extremely weak hand. I opened from the button with Qd-9c to 300. 'Pro' in the small blind has 3-bet me to 1000. K3o is a weak offsuit hand. Even on the button in a 3-handed game, it is generally better to fold rather than open-raise with such poor playability and kicker issues.

I am in the Small Blind with K-5 offsuit, heads-up against the Big Blind after the button folded. K-5 offsuit is a weak, easily dominated hand, especially out of position against a Big Blind raise. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my chip stack rather than chasing a marginal hand.

My hand, Queen of Diamonds and Four of Hearts, is too weak to play from the button against two opponents. Folding is the most disciplined and strategic decision to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I have Ace-Seven offsuit on the button, which is a strong starting hand in three-handed play. I will raise to three times the big blind to put pressure on the blinds and build the pot with a hand that likely has high equity against their ranges.

Queen-Six offsuit is a weak, disconnected hand. Facing a raise from the Button, playing this out of position from the Small Blind would be highly unprofitable. Folding is the standard and correct play.

I am in the small blind with a weak hand (8-6 offsuit). With the button (Pro) having folded, I am heads-up against the big blind. Holding 2s 5h on the button is a very weak hand with no playability. There is no strategic reason to invest chips into this pot given the strength of the starting hand. Folding is the standard and correct play.

I am in the Big Blind with pocket sixes (6c, 6s) after Flash (Button) raised to 300. With 200 chips required to call and 450 in the pot, the pot odds are excellent to play the pocket pair. I am out of position in the Big Blind against the Button on a board of 9d-8h-2h. Facing a continuation bet of 350 into a pot of 650 on a board of 9d-8h-2h. I am on the Button with 98o. I am in the small blind with a strong suited hand, Ah 7h. Since the button (Pro) folded, I am in a heads-up situation against the big blind (Flash). Raising is the standard aggressive play to take control of the pot and put pressure on the opponent, as suited aces perform well in this context.

I am in the Big Blind with 7s 2d, one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Flashlight, the small blind, has raised to 300. I have a premium starting hand, Ace-King offsuit, while in the button position. This is a strong opportunity to take control of the hand pre-flop and build the pot. A standard raise to 300 has been effective throughout the session.

With Ace-King offsuit in the big blind facing a button raise, I have a premium hand that plays very well as a 3-bet. A 3-bet out of position to around 1000 builds the pot with a strong hand, denies equity, and puts maximum pressure on the button's opening range.

Facing a 3-bet from the Big Blind, my AK offsuit is too strong to fold and plays very well in position. A 4-bet to 2500 is a standard, aggressive move that reclaims the initiative and forces the opponent to define their range, effectively building the pot while leveraging my positional advantage.

Facing a Button 4-bet, AK offsuit is a premium hand that is too strong to fold, but plays poorly as a flat call out of position since it misses the flop around 65% of the time. A 5-bet all-in jam puts maximum pressure on the opponent, forces out 4-bet bluffs, ensures we realize all our equity, and often flips favorably against pairs like QQ or JJ, while dominating hands like AQ.

With Ace-King offsuit and having already committed 2,500 of my 5,350 stack, I am effectively pot-committed to this hand. Jd6h is a very weak unsuited hand. Facing a raise from the Button, the correct play from the Small Blind is to fold.

Ace-Queen suited is a premium hand in a short-handed game. Since I am in the big blind and the button has opened, a 3-bet to 900 is a standard move to take control of the pot and isolate the button. This represents a strong range and puts pressure on the opponent.

I originally raised to 300 from the button with King-Four offsuit, and now I face a 3-bet to 900 from Flashlight in the big blind. Calling 600 more to see a flop with a weak King and poor kicker against a player who might be shoving or playing very aggressively post-flop is not ideal. Fold and wait for a better spot to exploit Flashlight's reduced stack.

Q7s is a marginal suited hand. While it can sometimes be opened from the button in a 3-handed game as a steal, the players in the blinds have been relatively aggressive with 3-bets recently. Folding is the safer and more solid choice to avoid tricky post-flop spots with weak top-pair potential.

Holding Ten-Four offsuit in the small blind is a very weak hand. Even though the price to call is only 50 chips to complete the big blind, playing out of position with such a weak holding against a stack that covers me is negative expected value. I will fold to preserve my remaining stack for a better spot.

I am on the button with Ace-Eight offsuit, a hand that holds decent equity in a late-position opening range. A raise to 300 is a standard sizing to build the pot and apply pressure.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Ten suited, which is a premium hand in a three-handed game. Flashlight has raised to 300 from the button. A 3-bet here allows me to take the initiative, potentially win the pot pre-flop, or build a value pot with a hand that has great playability. I will raise to 1000 total.

I opened the pot from the button with Ace-Eight offsuit, which is a standard steal attempt. Flash in the small blind 3-bet to 1000, which represents a strong range. I am on the button with Q4o. While Q4o is a relatively weak hand, opening from the button is generally profitable to steal the blinds or play in position. However, given my stack size and the desire to maintain a healthy chip lead, I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the hand.
I have Ten-Eight offsuit in the Small Blind against a Button open. This hand is too weak to call or 3-bet from out of position. The standard and optimal play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

Q4o is a weak hand that does not warrant defending the big blind against a raise from the button. The pot odds of 2:1 are not sufficient to justify calling with such a marginal range, especially out of position.
64o is a very weak, unsuited, disconnected hand. Even from the button in a 3-handed game, this hand has poor playability and equity. Folding is the mathematically correct and optimal decision.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Ten offsuit after the Button folded. Given the heads-up situation against the Big Blind, Jack-Ten is a playable hand. Completing the bet to see a flop is a standard approach here to keep the pot small and realize equity.

I have two pair (Kings and Tens) with a Jack kicker. The board is K-K-Q-T-8. I have Ace-high (Ad, 3d) on a board of Kc-Ts-Kh-Qd-8d. My best five-card hand is K-K-A-Q-T. I am on the button with A-3 suited, which is a strong enough hand to open from this position. A standard raise to 300 is appropriate to try and steal the blinds or play in position with the initiative.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Four suited, a hand that has good playability and equity against a Small Blind open. The pot odds offer a profitable call, and while I could re-raise, calling is the most balanced and lower-variance approach given the stack sizes and position. I will see a flop.

With Ace-high on a board of Tc-8s-Qd-3c-5s, I have a marginal hand with no real showdown value against a pair. I am on the Button with ATo in a 3-handed game. This is a premium hand in this position and a standard open raise for value and to steal the blinds. I will raise to 2.5x (250) to build a pot and put pressure on the blinds.
